item.title: The electromechanical angle computer inside the B-52 bomber's star tracker
item.description: Before GPS, how did aircraft navigate? One important technique was celestial navigation: navigating from the positions of the stars, planets...

item.title: Task Manager is LYING About Your CPU Usage (Here's the Truth)
item.description: Ever wonder why Task Manager shows a CPU value that doesn't "feel" right? Dave explains why Task Manager shows you the values it does and how they are determined. Comments with question marks get answered on our weekly ShopTalk: https://youtu.be/iT0T52OV9B8

item.title: Why Japan has such good railways - Works in Progress Magazine
item.description: Japan's railways are the finest in the world. Other countries can copy its formula.

item.title: State of Kdenlive - 2026
item.description: In 2025, the Kdenlive team continued grinding to push the project forward through steady development, collaboration, and community support. Over the past year we’ve found a nice balance between adding new features, bug fixing, polishing the user interface, and improving performance and workflow, with stability taking priority over feature creep.

item.title: Alex L. Zhang | The Mismanaged Geniuses Hypothesis
item.description: We propose the mismanaged geniuses hypothesis, which posits that existing frontier language models are severely underutilized due to sub-optimal use of individual language model calls.

item.description: How I built a playable DOOM MCP app that runs inline in compatible AI clients and falls back to a browser launch URL.

item.title: The Blast Radius Problem: Running Your Coding Agent in YOLO Mode
item.description: Running your coding agent in bypass mode on your laptop is the mistake. A VM with Tailscale and default-deny egress is how you shrink the blast radius.

item.title: Why LLMs Aren't Giving You the Result You Expect | Why I Prefer Claude Code Today
item.description: Every week somebody tells me 'I canceled my Claude plan, it just doesn't perform as well as GPT for me.' I've got 500+ hours in Claude Code and Codex, 400k lines generated, and both deliver. The difference isn't the model. It's how people are talking to it.

item.title: Running Your Own AS: Direct Hetzner Peering, a Fourth Edge, and Bringing the Home LAN into the Fabric
item.description: Part 4 of the AS201379 journey: adding a fourth FreeBSD edge router at iFog with FogIXP peering, establishing direct BGP sessions with Hetzner, bringing the home network into the AS via an iBGP-spe...

item.title: Caching Strategies from Scratch · Vaibhav Acharya, engineer.
item.description: LRU, LFU, TTL, write-through, write-back, and cache stampedes explained with interactive demos

item.title: HTTP desync in Discord's media proxy: Spying on a whole platform
item.description: In 2022, I came across a quirky behavior on media.discordapp.net when I miskeyed a space character into an attachment link: a 502 bad gateway. After some fiddling I realized that this was caused by a HTTP injection bug within the media proxy’s request to the upstream GCP bucket. The space character corrupted the proxied HTTP message, which caused the connection to prematurely terminate. For example, a crafted user request to the media proxy would look like this:

item.title: Teaching a Model to Code | Rig
item.description: Part 1 of our How We Built Rig series - building a data pipeline to teach a model to act like a real developer.

item.title: Closed Source is a Business Decision, not Security. | Thoughts by Javier
item.description: Cal.com claims that moving to closed source is a security decision. It is not.

item.title: Fonts I Love
item.description: My go-to font stack for everything I do, from my website to my projects.

item.title: Against big tech credentialism - alex wennerberg
item.description: Ryan Peterman is a YouTube interviewer who speaks with senior employees at big tech companies. On the one hand, it is incredible how accessible high-income professionals at highly competitive organizations are, and how open they are willing to speak about their work. But there is some danger in presuming that there is wisdom to be gleaned from them.

item.title: Many anti-AI arguments are conservative arguments
item.description: Most anti-AI rhetoric is left-wing coded. Popular criticisms of AI describe it as a tool of techno-fascism, or appeal to predominantly left-wing concerns like carbon emissions, democracy, or police brutality. Anti-AI sentiment is surprisingly bipartisan, but the big anti-AI institutions are labor unions and the progressive wing of the Democrats.

item.title: We Are All Playing Politics at Work
item.description:
Politics is any discussion where the truth doesn't steer the course of action.
Most of us like to think we are above it. We believe that in our daily jobs, we are rational actors exchanging facts. We

item.description: Why go/no-go decisions still run on faith in contracts, and the gap between pipelines that prove consistency and pipelines that produce evidence.

item.title: Pop!OS Linux: Creating a bootable Backup USB with Encryption
item.description: For my Pop!OS Linux workstation, I wanted to have a complete backup on an USB drive. Of course, it needs to be encrypted, in case the USB drive gets lots. And to allow me to quickly resume working in the case of an emergency, I wanted the USB backup to be bootable, too. Here’s how I set that up:

item.description: I finally got a monitor that supports DDC/CI, so now I can easily switch my monitor and full peripheral set with 1 button!

item.description: How we built Canon, a search pipeline orchestrator for full control over our search engine as it scales in complexity.
